log:
# Change log — Host-microbiota maladaptation in colorectal cancer

## 2026-06-27
- Ingested source: Janney, Powrie & Mann, "Host-microbiota maladaptation in colorectal cancer",
  *Nature* 585:509-517 (2020). Saved immutable copy at `raw/source.md`.
- Created 1 Publication source node anchored to `raw/source.md` (DOI:10.1038/s41586-020-2729-3).
- Authored 21 typed concept docs covering the review's key entities:
  - Disease: Colorectal cancer.
  - Anatomy: Colonic epithelium.
  - Organism: pks+ Escherichia coli, Enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis, Fusobacterium
    nucleatum, Clostridium butyricum.
  - Molecule: Colibactin, Bacteroides fragilis toxin, FadA adhesin, Fap2, Butyrate, Deoxycholic
    acid, Interleukin-22, E-cadherin, TIGIT, GPR109A.
  - BiologicalPathway: Wnt signaling pathway, NF-kB signaling pathway, IL-23/IL-17 inflammatory
    axis, Genotoxicity, Antitumor immune response.
  - BiologicalFunction: Histone deacetylase activity.
  - Phenotype: DNA double-strand breaks.
  - Concept: Gut microbiota dysbiosis.
  - Food: Dietary fiber.
  - Exposure: Low-fiber diet.
  - MethodOrProcedure: Gnotobiotic faecal transplant mouse model.
- Stamped every edge with the provenance triplet (knowledge_level / agent_type / primary_source),
  primary_source = the review's source node identifier; added reported_in edges to the source node.
- Updated index.md catalog.
name: host-microbiota-maladaptation-colorectal-cancer

Antitumor immune response.body:
# Antitumor immune response

The antitumor immune response is the host cytotoxic programme (natural killer and T cells) that
recognizes and eliminates transformed colonocytes. Tumour-associated microbiota subvert it:
*Fusobacterium nucleatum* Fap2 engages the inhibitory receptor TIGIT to suppress immune-cell
attack, helping colorectal tumours evade clearance (Gur et al., Immunity 2015, as reviewed by
Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Bacteroides fragilis toxin.body:
# Bacteroides fragilis toxin

The *Bacteroides fragilis* toxin (BFT, fragilysin) is a zinc-metalloprotease secreted by
enterotoxigenic *B. fragilis*. By targeting colonic epithelial cells it cleaves E-cadherin and
activates NF-κB, driving polarized secretion of CXC chemokines (e.g. IL-8) and TGF-β and
coordinating a pro-carcinogenic inflammatory cascade in the distal colon (Kim et al., Clin. Exp.
Immunol. 2001; Sanfilippo et al., Clin. Exp. Immunol. 2000; Chung et al., Cell Host Microbe 2018,
as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

Butyrate.body:
# Butyrate

Butyrate is a short-chain fatty acid produced by commensal bacteria fermenting dietary fiber. It
inhibits histone deacetylases (HDAC), signals through the receptor GPR109A (the niacin receptor),
and shapes intestinal macrophage function, suppressing colonic inflammation and carcinogenesis. In
gnotobiotic models, dietary fiber protects against colorectal tumorigenesis in a microbiota- and
butyrate-dependent manner (Donohoe et al., Cancer Discov. 2014; Singh et al., Immunity 2014;
Chang et al., PNAS 2014, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

Clostridium butyricum.body:
# Clostridium butyricum

*Clostridium butyricum* is a butyrate-producing commensal used as a probiotic. It inhibits
intestinal tumour development by modulating Wnt signalling and reshaping the gut microbiota,
illustrating a protective (anti-tumorigenic) member of the colonic community (Chen et al., Cancer
Lett. 2020, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Colibactin.body:
# Colibactin

Colibactin is a genotoxic secondary metabolite produced by *pks*+ *Escherichia coli*. It
alkylates DNA and induces double-strand breaks, generating a distinctive mutational signature
linked to established CRC driver mutations and promoting colon tumour growth via a
senescence-associated secretory phenotype (Cougnoux et al., Gut 2014; Pleguezuelos-Manzano et al.,
Nature 2020). Small-molecule inhibitors of colibactin biosynthesis prevent its genotoxic and
pro-tumoural effects (Cougnoux et al., Gut 2016, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

Colonic epithelium.body:
# Colonic epithelium

The single-cell layer of the intestinal epithelium that forms the barrier between the luminal
microbiota and host tissue. Its disruption is a central theme in microbiota-driven colorectal
carcinogenesis; the mucus layer overlying the colonic epithelium can be degraded by mucus-foraging
bacteria when dietary fiber is scarce (Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Colorectal cancer.body:
# Colorectal cancer

Colorectal cancer (CRC) is a heterogeneous disease of the intestinal epithelium characterized by
the accumulation of mutations and a dysregulated immune response. Up to ~90% of disease risk is
attributed to environmental factors such as diet. A growing body of literature describes an
'oncogenic' CRC-associated microbiota; whether this dysbiosis is causal or a bystander effect
remains under investigation (Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

DNA double-strand breaks.body:
# DNA double-strand breaks

DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs) are a severe form of DNA damage observed in colonic epithelial
cells exposed to microbial genotoxins. Colibactin from *pks*+ *E. coli* and secondary bile acids
induce DSBs and genomic instability in vivo, generating the mutational signatures that underlie
colorectal cancer driver mutations (Nougayrede et al., Science 2006; Cuevas-Ramos et al., PNAS
2010, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Deoxycholic acid.body:
# Deoxycholic acid

Deoxycholic acid (DCA) is a secondary bile acid generated by gut bacteria from host primary bile
acids. It is co-mutagenic and genotoxic to colonic epithelial cells and is implicated as a
microbiota-derived promoter of colorectal carcinogenesis, linking high-fat diets to CRC risk
(Shibuya et al., Mutat. Res. 1997, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

Dietary fiber.body:
# Dietary fiber

Dietary fiber is the fermentable plant carbohydrate that commensal bacteria convert into
short-chain fatty acids, principally butyrate. In gnotobiotic mouse models, dietary fiber protects
against colorectal tumorigenesis in a microbiota- and butyrate-dependent manner; conversely,
fiber deprivation shifts the microbiota toward mucus degradation and barrier breakdown (Donohoe et
al., Cancer Discov. 2014; Desai et al., Cell 2016, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

E-cadherin.body:
# E-cadherin

E-cadherin (CDH1) is an epithelial cell-adhesion protein that normally sequesters β-catenin and
restrains Wnt signalling at colonic epithelial junctions. Microbial ligands target E-cadherin:
*Fusobacterium nucleatum* FadA binds E-cadherin to release β-catenin and activate Wnt signalling,
and *Bacteroides fragilis* toxin cleaves E-cadherin, both promoting epithelial proliferation in
colorectal cancer (as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis.body:
# Enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis

Enterotoxigenic *Bacteroides fragilis* (ETBF) is a strain of *B. fragilis* that secretes the
*Bacteroides fragilis* toxin (BFT, fragilysin). ETBF drives a pro-carcinogenic inflammatory
cascade in the distal colon by inducing locally restricted chemokine expression and engaging the
IL-23/IL-17 inflammatory axis; polyamine catabolism contributes to ETBF-induced colon
tumorigenesis (Chung et al., Cell Host Microbe 2018; Goodwin et al., PNAS 2011, as reviewed by
Janney et al., Nature 2020).

FadA adhesin.body:
# FadA adhesin

FadA is a surface adhesin of *Fusobacterium nucleatum* that binds host E-cadherin on colonic
epithelial cells. This engagement activates Wnt/β-catenin signalling and promotes proliferation of
colorectal cancer cells, contributing to *F. nucleatum*-driven tumorigenesis (as reviewed by
Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Fap2.body:
# Fap2

Fap2 is a galactose-binding lectin/autotransporter of *Fusobacterium nucleatum*. By binding the
human inhibitory receptor TIGIT on natural killer and T cells, Fap2 suppresses immune cell
cytotoxicity and protects colorectal tumours from immune attack (Gur et al., Immunity 2015, as
reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Fusobacterium nucleatum.body:
# Fusobacterium nucleatum

*Fusobacterium nucleatum* is an oral commensal that is enriched in colorectal tumours, where it
potentiates intestinal tumorigenesis and modulates the tumour-immune microenvironment (Kostic et
al., Cell Host Microbe 2013). Its FadA adhesin engages host E-cadherin to activate Wnt/β-catenin
signalling, and its Fap2 lectin binds the inhibitory receptor TIGIT to shield tumours from immune
attack (Gur et al., Immunity 2015). *Fusobacterium* persists through CRC metastasis and antibiotic
treatment reduces tumour burden (Bullman et al., Science 2017, as reviewed by Janney et al.,
Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

GPR109A.body:
# GPR109A

GPR109A (HCAR2) is the G-protein-coupled receptor for niacin and the commensal metabolite
butyrate. Activation of GPR109A on colonic epithelium and immune cells suppresses colonic
inflammation and carcinogenesis, mediating part of butyrate's anti-tumour effect (Singh et al.,
Immunity 2014, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Genotoxicity.body:
# Genotoxicity

Genotoxicity is the process by which microbiota-derived agents (colibactin, secondary bile acids,
reactive oxygen species) damage host DNA in colonic epithelial cells, producing double-strand
breaks and mutational signatures that seed colorectal cancer driver mutations. Limiting oxidative
DNA damage reduces microbe-induced colitis-associated CRC (Irrazabal et al., Nat. Commun. 2020, as
reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Gnotobiotic faecal transplant mouse mode.body:
# Gnotobiotic faecal transplant mouse model

Gnotobiotic (germ-free) mice colonized with defined or patient-derived microbiota are the central
experimental system for establishing causality in microbiota-driven colorectal cancer. Gavaging
faecal samples from patients with CRC into germ-free or conventional mice promotes intestinal
carcinogenesis, while gnotobiotic models also demonstrate fiber- and butyrate-dependent protection
(Wong et al., Gastroenterology 2017; Donohoe et al., Cancer Discov. 2014, as reviewed by Janney et
al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Gut microbiota dysbiosis.body:
# Gut microbiota dysbiosis

Gut microbiota dysbiosis denotes the altered, 'oncogenic' CRC-associated microbial community
described across the literature. Faecal transplantation of stools from patients with CRC into
germ-free or conventional mice promotes intestinal carcinogenesis, illustrating a carcinogenic
role for this dysbiotic community; whether dysbiosis is causal or a bystander effect of
established tumours remains an open question (Wong et al., Gastroenterology 2017, as reviewed by
Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Histone deacetylase activity.body:
# Histone deacetylase activity

Histone deacetylase (HDAC) activity removes acetyl groups from histones, repressing transcription
and influencing colonocyte proliferation. The commensal metabolite butyrate is an HDAC inhibitor;
this inhibition underlies much of butyrate's regulation of intestinal macrophage function and its
anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic effect in the colon (Chang et al., PNAS 2014, as reviewed by
Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

IL-23/IL-17 inflammatory axis.body:
# IL-23/IL-17 inflammatory axis

The IL-23/IL-17 (Th17) axis is a pro-tumorigenic inflammatory programme triggered when adenoma
barrier defects allow microbial products to penetrate the epithelium. IL-23 from myeloid cells
drives IL-17-producing Th17 cells, fuelling tumour growth in colorectal cancer; enterotoxigenic
*B. fragilis* is a potent inducer of this axis (Grivennikov et al., Nature 2012, as reviewed by
Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Interleukin-22.body:
# Interleukin-22

Interleukin-22 (IL-22) is a cytokine that acts on the intestinal epithelium to promote barrier
function and protect intestinal stem cells against genotoxic stress. Dietary stimuli mediate IL-22
signalling, which can shield stem cells from genotoxic insults; however, dysregulated IL-22 in the
context of chronic inflammation can also contribute to tumour growth, making its role
context-dependent (Gronke et al., Nature 2019; Perez et al., Nat. Commun. 2020, as reviewed by
Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptatio.body:
# Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Review by Alina Janney, Fiona Powrie and Elizabeth H. Mann, *Nature* **585**, 509-517 (2020),
"Host-microbiota maladaptation in colorectal cancer". The review synthesizes host-microbiota
interactions in colorectal cancer (CRC), focusing on mechanisms linked to intestinal barrier
disruption, genotoxicity and deleterious inflammation. It notes that up to 90% of CRC risk is
attributed to environmental factors such as diet, consistent with an 'oncogenic' CRC-associated
microbiota whose causal vs. bystander role remains under investigation.

This node is the in-bundle source anchor; its immutable bytes are at `raw/source.md`.

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptatio.identifier: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

Low-fiber diet.body:
# Low-fiber diet

A low-fiber (Western-style) diet deprives the gut microbiota of fermentable substrate. The
fiber-starved community turns to foraging the colonic mucus layer, degrading the mucus barrier,
enhancing pathogen susceptibility and promoting aggressive colitis, thereby predisposing to
colorectal cancer (Desai et al., Cell 2016, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

NF-kB signaling pathway.body:
# NF-kB signaling pathway

NF-κB is a master pro-inflammatory transcriptional pathway in colonic epithelial and immune cells.
*Bacteroides fragilis* toxin activates NF-κB to drive polarized CXC-chemokine (IL-8) and TGF-β
expression, linking microbial enterotoxins to the deleterious inflammation that promotes
colorectal cancer (Kim et al., Clin. Exp. Immunol. 2001, as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature
2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

TIGIT.body:
# TIGIT

TIGIT (T cell immunoreceptor with Ig and ITIM domains) is a human inhibitory immune-checkpoint
receptor on natural killer and T cells. *Fusobacterium nucleatum* Fap2 binds TIGIT to dampen
cytotoxic activity, allowing colorectal tumours to evade immune attack (Gur et al., Immunity 2015,
as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

edges.object: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review
edges.primary_source: Janney 2020 host-microbiota maladaptation in CRC review

synonyms: T cell immunoreceptor with Ig and ITIM domains

Wnt signaling pathway.body:
# Wnt signaling pathway

The Wnt/β-catenin signalling pathway controls intestinal stem-cell proliferation and is a
canonical driver of colorectal carcinogenesis. Microbiota can hijack this pathway: *F. nucleatum*
FadA activates Wnt via E-cadherin, while butyrate-producing probiotics such as *Clostridium
butyricum* modulate Wnt to suppress tumour development (as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020).

pks+ Escherichia coli.body:
# pks+ Escherichia coli

Strains of *Escherichia coli* that carry the *pks* (polyketide synthase) genomic island and
produce the genotoxin colibactin. These bacteria induce DNA double-strand breaks and a specific
mutational signature in colonic epithelial cells, providing direct evidence of causality between
pks+ *E. coli* and established CRC driver mutations (Pleguezuelos-Manzano et al., Nature 2020,
as reviewed by Janney et al., Nature 2020). Colibactin-producing *E. coli* promote colon tumour
growth, and small-molecule inhibitors can prevent these genotoxic and pro-tumoural effects.
